1

The Four Macroeconomic Objectives — A Recap

Before exploring conflicts, it is essential to recall what the government and the Bank of England are actually trying to achieve. AQA's macroeconomic framework rests on four core objectives. Most exam questions about "policy conflicts" are really asking you to explain why progress on one objective can come at the expense of another.

Low & Stable Inflation
CPI inflation at the government's 2% target. Price stability allows households and firms to plan, preserves the value of savings, and maintains the UK's international competitiveness.
Low Unemployment
Minimising involuntary unemployment so the economy operates close to full employment, ideally with unemployment at or near the NAIRU (Non-Accelerating Inflation Rate of Unemployment) — see Section 7.
Economic Growth
Sustained increases in real GDP over time, ideally driven by growth in productive potential (LRAS) rather than short-term demand surges that risk overheating.
Balance of Payments Equilibrium
Avoiding a large, persistent current account deficit (importing far more than is exported) or a destabilising surplus, and keeping the exchange rate broadly stable.
Secondary Objectives — Increasingly Examined: AQA's core framework centres on the four objectives above, but a genuinely strong answer recognises that policymakers also weigh several closely-related secondary objectives that can conflict with the core four just as sharply: environmental sustainability (growth can come at the cost of higher emissions and resource depletion), government finances (expansionary policy to support growth/employment worsens the budget deficit and national debt), and inequality and living standards (aggregate growth does not guarantee that gains are shared, and some growth-boosting supply-side policies can widen the gap between winners and losers). These are explored in full, with real-world examples and supply-side solutions, in Section 9 (Conflicts 5–7).

Why Conflicts Arise

In an ideal world, all four objectives would move together. In practice, the short-run relationship between them is often a trade-off: a policy that improves one objective frequently worsens another. The single most important organising idea in this topic is the distinction between the short run (where trade-offs are common) and the long run (where, with a healthy supply side, all four objectives can in principle be achieved simultaneously).

How to Read This Guide: this guide builds the case step by step — first the concept of the output gap (Sections 2–5), which links AD fluctuations to both unemployment and inflation; then the Phillips Curve (Sections 6–8), the most famous expression of the inflation–unemployment conflict; then the policy conflicts themselves and how they might be reconciled (Sections 9–12); then formal AD/AS analysis (Section 13); and finally evaluation and exam technique (Sections 14–16).
★ REAL WORLD: The UK's Macroeconomic Scorecard
At various points since 2008, the UK has faced almost every possible combination of these objectives moving in opposite directions: 2009 saw collapsing growth and rising unemployment but falling inflation (textbook negative output gap); 2022 saw CPI inflation reach 11.1% while unemployment stayed historically low (a supply-shock conflict); and the 2010s saw record employment alongside weak productivity growth — illustrating that the objectives rarely move in lockstep.
2

Output Gaps — The Foundation Concept

The output gap is the single most important link between aggregate demand, unemployment and inflation. Almost every policy conflict in this topic can be traced back to the size and direction of the output gap.

Potential Output (Yfc)
The level of real GDP the economy can produce when all factors of production are employed at their "normal" or sustainable rate — sometimes called full-capacity output or trend output. This is shown by the position of LRAS on an AD/AS diagram.
Actual Output (Y)
The level of real GDP the economy is currently producing, determined by the intersection of AD and SRAS.
Output Gap
The percentage difference between actual output and potential output: Output Gap = (Actual Output − Potential Output) ÷ Potential Output × 100.
Negative Output Gap
Actual output is below potential — the economy has spare capacity. Associated with rising unemployment and falling/low inflation.
Positive Output Gap
Actual output is above potential — the economy is overheating. Associated with falling unemployment (below the NAIRU) and rising inflation.
The Key Causal Chain You Must Be Able to Reproduce: change in AD → change in the output gap → change in unemployment → change in inflationary pressure. Examiners reward answers that explicitly name the output gap as the transmission link between a demand shock and its effects on the labour market and prices — don't jump straight from "AD rises" to "inflation rises" without mentioning the gap.

Why the Output Gap Drives Both Unemployment and Inflation

When AD changes relative to potential output, two things happen simultaneously, because both unemployment and inflation are determined by the same gap between demand and supply capacity:

Labour market effect: Output is produced using labour. If firms are producing below potential (negative gap), they need fewer workers than the economy's workforce can supply — cyclical unemployment rises. If firms are producing above sustainable potential (positive gap), they draw in extra workers, including some who would otherwise be frictionally unemployed. Some workers previously classified as structurally unemployed may also re-enter employment as firms become more willing to offer training or relax recruitment criteria to fill vacancies — pushing unemployment below the NAIRU.

Price level effect: A negative output gap means weak demand relative to supply capacity — firms have spare capacity and little pricing power, so inflationary pressure eases (demand-deficient disinflation). A positive output gap means demand is outstripping the economy's capacity to produce — firms face rising costs (overtime, scarce inputs, bidding up wages for scarce labour) and can pass these on as higher prices, generating demand-pull inflation.

★ REAL WORLD: The Output Gap and the 2008–09 Recession
The OBR estimated the UK output gap fell to around −4% of potential GDP at the trough of the 2008–09 financial crisis — one of the largest negative output gaps in postwar UK history. Unemployment rose from 5.2% in early 2008 to a peak of 8.5% in 2011, while CPI inflation, having spiked on import and commodity prices in 2008, fell back sharply as the negative gap took hold domestically.
3

The Big Picture — How It All Connects

Before going further, it helps to see the whole chain in one place. Every policy conflict in this topic — whatever section it appears in — is really a version of the same underlying sequence: a change in AD opens an output gap, the output gap moves unemployment and inflation in opposite directions (the Phillips Curve trade-off), and policymakers must then decide how to respond.

Fig 3.1 — The complete chain from a change in AD through to the Phillips Curve trade-off. The coral (left) path shows an overheating economy; the navy (right) path shows a recession. Both converge on the short-run trade-off — manageable via demand-side policy (Section 10) and ultimately resolvable via supply-side policy (Section 11).
How to Use This Diagram: whenever you are asked to explain a policy conflict, try to place the scenario in the question somewhere on this chain. Ask: is this a positive or negative output gap? Has the question already reached the Phillips Curve trade-off, or is it still describing the AD shock that caused it? Identifying your position on the chain tells you which section's chain of reasoning (Sections 4, 5, 7, 10 or 11) to borrow from in your answer.
4

Positive Output Gaps & Demand-Pull Inflation

A positive output gap represents an economy that is overheating — growing faster than its sustainable productive capacity allows.

1
Aggregate demand rises faster than potential output

A surge in consumption, investment, government spending, or net exports — perhaps driven by low interest rates, fiscal stimulus, or a credit boom — shifts AD to the right faster than LRAS is growing.

2
A positive output gap opens up

Actual output rises above potential output. The economy is producing beyond its sustainable, non-inflationary capacity — using overtime, running machinery harder, and drawing on workers who would otherwise be frictionally unemployed.

3
Unemployment falls below the NAIRU

Firms compete for increasingly scarce labour. Job seekers find work faster (shorter frictional unemployment), and some workers previously classified as structurally unemployed may re-enter employment as firms become more willing to offer training or relax recruitment criteria.

4
Labour and input markets tighten — costs rise

Scarce labour means workers can bargain for higher wages; scarce capacity means firms compete for limited raw materials, components and machine time, bidding up input costs.

5
Firms pass on rising costs as higher prices

With demand strong and capacity scarce, firms have pricing power — they can raise prices without losing significant sales volume, since customers have few alternatives and are competing for the same limited output.

6
Demand-pull inflation rises, potentially above the 2% target

CPI inflation accelerates. If the MPC judges this to be sustained rather than temporary, it faces pressure to raise the Bank Rate to cool demand back towards potential output (see Section 10).

Fig 4.1 — A positive output gap: AD intersects SRAS to the right of LRAS, leaving the economy producing above potential output (Y) at price level P, reflecting demand-pull inflationary pressure.
The Critical Conflict Embedded Here: this is where the textbook short-run policy conflict lives: a positive output gap delivers low unemployment (good for the unemployment objective) but rising inflation (bad for the price stability objective). A policymaker who is happy with low unemployment may be uncomfortable with the inflation that increasingly accompanies it the longer the gap persists. This is the real-world content behind the Phillips Curve (Sections 6–8).
★ REAL WORLD: Overheating Before the 2008 Crisis
In the years immediately before the 2008 financial crisis, UK unemployment fell to around 5.0% (below most estimates of the NAIRU at the time) while credit growth and house prices boomed. Some economists argue this reflected a positive output gap driven by cheap credit and rising household debt — a boom that proved unsustainable once the financial crisis hit and the gap reversed sharply into negative territory.
5

Negative Output Gaps, Unemployment & Disinflation

A negative output gap is the defining feature of a recession or a sluggish recovery. Examiners expect a precise, step-by-step explanation of how it generates both higher unemployment and lower inflation — not just an assertion that "a recession causes unemployment".

1
Aggregate demand falls (or grows more slowly than potential output)

A fall in consumption, investment, government spending, or net exports — or a slowdown in their growth relative to the growth of potential output — shifts AD to the left relative to LRAS.

2
A negative output gap opens up

Actual output (where AD intersects SRAS) falls below potential output (LRAS). Firms are producing below their sustainable capacity.

3
Firms cut back on labour — cyclical (demand-deficient) unemployment rises

With weaker order books, firms reduce hours, freeze recruitment, and make redundancies. Derived demand for labour falls because demand for the goods and services labour produces has fallen.

4
Spare capacity removes firms' pricing power

Firms compete for a smaller pool of demand. Many cut prices, discount, or hold prices steady rather than raising them, to maintain sales volumes and market share.

5
Wage growth slows

Higher unemployment weakens workers' bargaining power. With more people competing for fewer vacancies, wage settlements fall, easing a key cost pressure on firms.

6
Inflation falls — possibly towards or below the 2% target

The combined effect of weak pricing power and slower wage growth pulls CPI inflation down. If the negative output gap is large and persistent, the risk of damaging deflation emerges.

Fig 5.1 — A negative output gap: AD intersects SRAS to the left of LRAS, leaving the economy producing below potential output (Y) at price level P, with spare capacity easing inflationary pressure.
Why This Matters for "Conflict" Questions: notice that in this scenario, unemployment and inflation move in the same direction relative to target (both worsen as the gap turns negative, or both improve as the gap closes) — they are not in conflict with each other here. The genuine policy conflict in a recession is usually between growth/unemployment objectives and the balance of payments (Section 9, Conflict 4) or, after a demand stimulus, the risk of reigniting inflation too soon. The famous unemployment–inflation trade-off (Sections 6–8) really bites when the economy is already close to full capacity.
★ REAL WORLD: Disinflation Without a Recession — 2023–24
UK CPI inflation fell from a peak of 11.1% (October 2022) to around 2% by mid-2024 without unemployment rising anywhere near as much as the 2008–09 episode. This was because much of the 2021–23 inflation surge was cost-push (energy and supply-chain driven) rather than purely demand-pull — so disinflation came partly from falling commodity prices and easing supply bottlenecks, not only from a negative output gap and rising unemployment.
6

The Original Phillips Curve

The Phillips Curve is the most famous diagram in this whole topic, and a thorough understanding of both its original (1958) version and Milton Friedman's later refinement (Section 7) is essential for top marks on policy conflict questions.

A.W. Phillips (1958)
A New Zealand economist who plotted UK data on wage inflation against unemployment for the period 1861–1957, and found a clear inverse relationship between the two.
The Original Finding
When unemployment was low, wage (and price) inflation tended to be high; when unemployment was high, wage inflation tended to be low or negative. The relationship appeared statistically stable over almost a century of data.
The Policy Implication (1960s view)
Governments appeared to face a genuine menu of choices — they could choose a point on the curve representing their preferred combination of inflation and unemployment, accepting more of one to get less of the other.
"Moving Along the Curve"
The phrase used to describe a government using demand management (fiscal or monetary stimulus, or contraction) to shift the economy to a different point on the same, fixed Phillips Curve.
Fig 6.1 — The Short-Run Phillips Curve (SRPC): as unemployment falls from U1 towards U4, inflation rises from P1 towards P4, accelerating sharply as unemployment approaches — and briefly dips below — the NAIRU (positioned at U3).

Linking the Phillips Curve to the Output Gap

The Phillips Curve is really just a different way of presenting the output gap analysis from Sections 2–5: as the economy moves towards a positive output gap (lower unemployment), demand-pull inflationary pressure rises — this traces out the curve. As the economy moves towards a negative output gap (higher unemployment), inflationary pressure eases — this traces out the curve in the other direction.

Why the Simple Story Eventually Broke Down: the original Phillips Curve relationship appeared to collapse in the 1970s, when many developed economies (including the UK) experienced stagflation — simultaneously rising inflation and rising unemployment, something the simple downward-sloping curve said should be impossible. This is what motivated Milton Friedman's revision in Section 7.
★ REAL WORLD: The 1970s Stagflation Crisis
Following the 1973 oil price shock, UK inflation reached over 24% in 1975 while unemployment also rose, eventually exceeding one million for the first time since the 1930s. A simple downward-sloping Phillips Curve could not explain inflation and unemployment rising together — the relationship had visibly shifted outward rather than staying fixed, discrediting the idea of a stable, exploitable trade-off.
7

Friedman, Expectations & the Long-Run Phillips Curve

Milton Friedman (and, independently, Edmund Phelps) resolved the puzzle of stagflation by introducing inflationary expectations into the model, and by distinguishing sharply between the short run and the long run.

NAIRU
The Non-Accelerating Inflation Rate of Unemployment — the rate of unemployment at which inflation is stable (neither accelerating nor decelerating). Equivalent to the equilibrium (NAIRU) rate of unemployment, made up of frictional and structural unemployment.
Short-Run Phillips Curve (SRPC)
A downward-sloping curve showing the trade-off between unemployment and inflation for a given level of inflationary expectations. There is a different SRPC for every expected inflation rate.
Long-Run Phillips Curve (LRPC)
A vertical line drawn at the NAIRU. It shows that in the long run, once expectations adjust, any rate of inflation is compatible with the same NAIRU — there is no long-run trade-off.
Money Illusion
The mistaken belief that a rise in money wages is a rise in real wages, when in fact prices have risen by the same amount. Friedman argued workers initially suffer money illusion but later realise their real wage hasn't changed.
Adaptive Expectations
The idea that people base their expectations of future inflation on past inflation experience, adjusting gradually as actual inflation surprises them.
Fig 7.1 — Two short-run Phillips Curves: SRPC1 (2% expected inflation) and a curve reflecting higher (6%) expected inflation. At the same NAIRU level of unemployment (U3), higher expected inflation shifts the whole curve upward — inflation is higher (P5 rather than P3) at the same rate of unemployment.

The Story Behind the Diagram, Step by Step

A
Starting point: economy at the NAIRU, 0% inflation, 0% expected inflation

The economy sits at 6% unemployment (its NAIRU) with stable 0% inflation. Workers and firms expect 0% inflation to continue.

B
A rise in AD creates a positive output gap — movement along Ph0

Firms raise wages to attract scarce labour. Workers, suffering money illusion, see higher money wages and believe their real reward from work has risen, so they shorten job search and accept jobs faster. Unemployment falls temporarily below the NAIRU as the economy moves along the short-run curve Ph0 to point B.

C
In the long run, workers realise prices have risen too — unemployment returns to the NAIRU, but on a higher SRPC

Workers realise their real wage hasn't actually improved — both wages and prices rose together. Unemployment rises back to the NAIRU (6%). But the actual inflation generated along the way raises inflationary expectations to 2%, shifting the whole short-run curve upward to Ph1. The economy is now at point C.

D–E
The process can repeat, ratcheting expectations ever higher

A further AD boost pushes the economy below the NAIRU again to point D (now on Ph1), injecting still higher inflation (5%). In the long run unemployment returns to the NAIRU at point E, but now on an even higher curve, Ph2. The NAIRU (6%) is compatible with any rate of inflation, depending only on what inflation is expected.

F
Disinflation requires a temporary rise in unemployment above the NAIRU

To bring inflation back down from 5% (point E) to 2% (point C) requires the economy to pass through point F — higher short-run unemployment than the NAIRU — until wage and price inflation falls and expectations adjust down to the lower curve.

The Key Conclusion — Learn This Sentence: "There is a short-run trade-off between inflation and unemployment whenever the economy moves away from the NAIRU due to fluctuations in aggregate demand, but there is no long-run trade-off — in the long run, the economy always returns to the NAIRU regardless of the inflation rate, because the NAIRU is determined by real, structural features of the labour market (frictions, immobilities, information failures) that a change in AD cannot fix."
★ REAL WORLD: Disinflation Under Volcker (US, 1979–82)
When Federal Reserve Chair Paul Volcker raised US interest rates aggressively to break double-digit inflation, unemployment rose sharply to over 10% — a real-world equivalent of the move through "point F". Inflation expectations did eventually fall, allowing both inflation and (later) unemployment to come down together, illustrating the painful short-run cost of disinflation predicted by the Friedman model.
8

Has the Phillips Curve Gone Flat?

In the 2010s and again in the mid-2020s, UK unemployment fell to multi-decade lows without the inflation surge that Friedman's model would predict — reviving the debate over whether the short-run Phillips Curve relationship has weakened. The inflation surge of 2021–23 reminded economists that the relationship may have weakened, but it has not disappeared entirely.

The Puzzle

As UK unemployment fell towards 4% (a 45-year low) in the late 2010s, economists repeatedly asked: Where is the inflation? What is the NAIRU now? When will inflation accelerate? Some went as far as to ask whether "the Phillips Curve is dead". The Bank of England cares about this a great deal, because if the curve really has flattened, the MPC can tolerate much lower unemployment before it needs to raise interest rates to prevent demand-pull inflation.

ReasonExplanation
Labour Market FlexibilityReforms (e.g. easier hiring/firing, weaker collective bargaining structures, more part-time and flexible contracts) have reduced frictional and structural unemployment, lowering the NAIRU itself — this shifts the whole LRPC left rather than flattening the SRPC (see Section 11).
Weaker Trade Unions & the Gig EconomyDeclining union membership and the rise of zero-hours and gig-economy contracts have reduced workers' bargaining power. Even with low unemployment, employees are less able (or willing) to push for large wage increases — many prioritise job security over pay rises.
GlobalisationImmigration has increased labour supply in many sectors; competition from low-cost economies and the threat of outsourcing/offshoring make domestic workers and firms reluctant to push through large wage or price increases for fear of losing competitiveness.
Anchored Inflation ExpectationsCredible, independent inflation targeting (see the companion BIZ-OMICS Monetary Policy guide) has kept inflationary expectations "anchored" near 2% for over two decades, muting the wage-price spiral that used to accompany falling unemployment.
Most Economists Don't Think the Curve Is Dead: the mainstream view is that the relationship still exists but has become flatter (a given fall in unemployment now produces a smaller rise in inflation than it used to) rather than disappearing altogether. If unemployment keeps falling far enough, inflation is still expected to "take off" eventually — the curve has shifted and flattened, not vanished.
Fig 8.1 — A flatter short-run Phillips Curve: SRPC2 (the original, steeper curve) and SRPC1 (the flatter curve seen in recent years) genuinely cross at the same point — the same inflation rate (I1) and estimated NAIRU (U1) — but as unemployment falls further below the NAIRU (moving left), SRPC1 stays much closer to I1 while SRPC2 accelerates away sharply.
Exam Tip: Distinguish "Flatter Curve" from "Lower NAIRU": these are different effects that are easy to confuse. A flatter SRPC means unemployment can fall further before inflation accelerates noticeably, but the NAIRU itself (where the vertical LRPC sits) may be unchanged. A fall in the NAIRU means the whole vertical long-run curve shifts left — the equilibrium (NAIRU) rate of unemployment is genuinely lower. Both can raise marks if clearly distinguished and not conflated.
★ REAL WORLD: Pre-COVID UK — A 45-Year Low in Unemployment
UK unemployment fell to around 3.8% in 2019 — its lowest rate since the early 1970s — while CPI inflation remained close to the 2% target. The Bank of England had been planning very gradual Bank Rate rises through 2019–21 in anticipation that demand-pull pressure would eventually emerge, but the flatness of the curve meant this pressure was slow to materialise, before the COVID-19 pandemic disrupted the picture entirely.
9

Short-Run Policy Conflicts — Seven Classic Cases

The specification asks you to explain how negative and positive output gaps relate to unemployment and inflation, and to analyse policy conflicts that result. Beyond the general Phillips Curve trade-off, seven specific conflict scenarios appear repeatedly in AQA exam questions and examiner reports — the first three centre on the core inflation/unemployment trade-off, while the remaining four extend the analysis to balance of payments, the environment, government finances, and inequality.

Conflict 1: Innovation, Growth & Structural Unemployment

Productivity-boosting innovation (automation, AI, robotics) is good for long-run economic growth — it shifts LRAS to the right, raising potential output without inflationary pressure. But in the short run it can destroy jobs in the industries being automated faster than new jobs are created elsewhere, generating structural unemployment as displaced workers' skills no longer match the vacancies on offer (occupational immobility — see the companion BIZ-OMICS Unemployment guide).

Innovation/automation introduced → labour-saving technology replaces workers in affected industries → structural unemployment rises in the short run (skills mismatch) → BUT productive capacity (LRAS) rises → long-run growth objective is served → over time, new industries and job types emerge, though retraining and time are required to re-employ displaced workers.

Conflict 2: Supply-Side Shocks & the Inflation–Output Stabilisation Trade-Off

A negative supply-side shock (e.g. an oil price spike) shifts SRAS left, simultaneously raising the price level and reducing real output — generating both higher inflation and higher unemployment together (stagflation). The central bank then faces a genuine dilemma:

Option A — Raise Rates to Fight Inflation

Bank Rate rises → AD falls further → the negative output gap widens → unemployment rises further → recession deepens, but inflation is brought under control faster.

Option B — Hold Rates to Support Output/Employment

Bank Rate held → AD not further reduced → output and employment are protected in the short run → but inflation remains above target for longer, and the 2% target may be temporarily overshot while the MPC prioritises growth/employment stabilisation.

The MPC's mandate explicitly allows for this: it must hit the 2% inflation target, but subject to that, it should support the government's growth and employment objectives — meaning it can choose to tolerate a temporary inflation overshoot rather than crush output to hit the target immediately (see the companion BIZ-OMICS Monetary Policy guide's supply-side shocks section).

Conflict 3: The Phillips Curve Trade-Off Itself

As covered in Sections 6–8: in the short run, reducing unemployment below the NAIRU (via demand stimulus) generates rising inflation; reducing inflation back down requires accepting higher unemployment, at least temporarily, until expectations adjust. This is the most exam-famous version of the conflict, and the one most likely to be explicitly named in a question.

Common Exam Phrasing to Recognise: questions often use the word "inevitably" — e.g. "Policies to reduce unemployment inevitably result in higher inflation. Discuss." This wording is deliberately inviting you to challenge the inevitability (see Section 12) rather than simply agree. A strong answer explains the conflict and then shows the circumstances in which it does not have to hold.
★ REAL WORLD: The 2022 Energy Shock Dilemma
Following Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022, a sharp rise in wholesale gas prices pushed UK CPI inflation above 11% while simultaneously squeezing real incomes and weakening growth. The MPC raised the Bank Rate steadily through 2022–23 despite a weak growth outlook, judging that the risk of inflation expectations becoming de-anchored outweighed the short-run cost to output — a real-world example of Option A above.

Conflict 4: Growth vs Balance of Payments

The first three conflicts in this section all centre on unemployment and inflation. But the introduction to this guide named four macroeconomic objectives, and the fourth — balance of payments equilibrium — deserves its own treatment, because it can conflict with growth and employment even when inflation is well behaved.

A demand-led expansion that successfully raises growth and lowers unemployment tends, at the same time, to widen the current account deficit. This is because rising domestic incomes spill over into demand for imports, while the goods and services driving the boom are not necessarily the ones the rest of the world wants to buy.

AD rises (consumption- or investment-led) → households' and firms' real incomes rise → demand for imported goods and services rises (assuming a positive marginal propensity to import) → if export growth does not keep pace, the trade balance worsens → the current account moves further into deficit → growth and employment improve, but the balance of payments objective deteriorates.
Why Growth Often Worsens the Current Account
The UK has a relatively high marginal propensity to import: a meaningful share of any rise in household spending leaks abroad rather than supporting domestic output. A demand-led boom therefore tends to pull in more imports without a matching rise in exports, widening the deficit even though growth and unemployment are both improving.
Why This Is a Genuine Conflict, Not Just a Side Effect
A persistent, large current account deficit must be financed — typically by attracting foreign capital inflows or running down reserves. If financing becomes harder to attract (e.g. if foreign investors lose confidence), the currency can depreciate sharply, importing inflation and forcing a disruptive correction. Policymakers cannot simply ignore the objective while chasing growth.
The Export-Led Alternative
Growth driven by rising net exports (rather than domestic consumption) raises GDP and employment while improving the current account — the objectives are reconciled. This is why governments often prefer export- or investment-led growth to consumption-led growth: it avoids the trade-off described above.

Linking Back to Supply-Side Policy: as with the unemployment–inflation trade-off, this conflict is more acute in the short run than the long run. Supply-side policies that raise competitiveness — investment in productive capacity, innovation, and improvements to international competitiveness (see the BIZ-OMICS Fiscal Policy guide's supply-side section, and the BIZ-OMICS Exchange Rates guide's discussion of export competitiveness) — allow the economy to grow and export more, easing the conflict between growth and the balance of payments over time (see Section 11). Specifically: investment in R&D and non-price competitiveness (product quality, branding, innovation) helps exporters compete on more than just price; infrastructure investment in ports, digital connectivity, and logistics lowers the cost of getting UK goods to foreign markets; and education and skills investment raises the productivity — and therefore the international competitiveness — of UK-based production, reducing the economy's reliance on imports for goods it could otherwise make competitively at home (import substitution).

★ REAL WORLD: The UK's Current Account Deficit vs Germany's Surplus
The UK has run a current account deficit in almost every year since the mid-1980s, standing at 2.2% of GDP in 2024 and 2.4% in 2025. This reflects a large trade in goods deficit (7.3% of GDP in 2024, driven substantially by decades of deindustrialisation and a shrinking manufacturing export base) only partly offset by a trade in services surplus (6.4% of GDP, led by financial and business services). Germany presents the sharpest possible contrast: it recorded the largest current account surplus in the G7 in 2024, at 5.8% of GDP, reflecting decades of sustained supply-side investment in export-oriented advanced manufacturing (the "Mittelstand" of globally competitive mid-sized exporters), strong vocational training feeding skilled labour into that sector, and a high domestic savings rate. The comparison illustrates the guide's central point directly: Germany's growth has historically been far more reconcilable with balance of payments equilibrium than the UK's, precisely because it is disproportionately export- and investment-led rather than consumption-led.

Conflict 5: Economic Growth vs Environmental Sustainability

Traditional GDP growth has historically been closely correlated with rising carbon emissions, resource depletion, and pollution — leading many economists to treat environmental sustainability as an increasingly important "fifth objective" alongside the traditional four, even though it does not appear in AQA's core objective list explicitly.

AD and/or LRAS rise, driving economic growth → higher output typically requires more energy and raw material use → without policy intervention, this raises carbon emissions and other environmental externalities → GDP and living standards rise in narrow monetary terms, but environmental damage (climate change, air and water pollution, biodiversity loss) imposes costs not captured in GDP itself → the growth objective is met, but at the expense of environmental sustainability, and potentially at the expense of the wellbeing of future generations.
The Environmental Kuznets Curve: some economists argue pollution follows an inverted-U shape as an economy develops — rising during early industrialisation, then falling once rising incomes shift demand towards cleaner services, generate political demand for environmental regulation, and provide the resources to invest in cleaner technology. This is contested, and even where per-capita or territorial emissions fall in developed economies, this can partly reflect "offshoring" of dirty production to developing countries rather than a genuine global improvement — a country's own falling emissions can simply mean it now imports the goods whose production still generates emissions elsewhere.

Supply-Side Reconciliation — "Green Growth": rather than treating growth and the environment as permanently opposed, well-designed supply-side policy can shift LRAS to the right in an environmentally sustainable direction. Government-backed R&D subsidies and investment incentives for renewable energy and clean technology raise long-run productive capacity while reducing the carbon intensity of that growth; carbon pricing (a tax or a cap-and-trade permit scheme) internalises the negative externality of emissions, incentivising firms to invest in cleaner production methods as a matter of ordinary profit-maximising behaviour rather than relying on goodwill.

★ REAL WORLD: Denmark's Offshore Wind Industry
Following the 1970s oil price shocks, the Danish government began decades of sustained public investment and R&D support for wind energy, initially framed around energy security rather than climate policy. This long-term supply-side commitment allowed Danish firms — most notably Ørsted, now one of the world's largest offshore wind developers — to build a globally competitive export industry from what began as a niche technology. Denmark now generates the majority of its electricity from wind, and wind turbine manufacturing and offshore wind expertise are significant UK and global export earners for the Danish economy — a clear real-world case of growth, exports, and environmental objectives being reconciled through decades of targeted supply-side investment, rather than treated as an unavoidable trade-off.
★ REAL WORLD: The EU Emissions Trading Scheme (ETS)
Launched in 2005, the EU ETS is a market-based, cap-and-trade carbon pricing scheme covering power generation and heavy industry: a shrinking overall cap on emissions is divided into tradeable permits, forcing the most polluting firms to either reduce emissions or pay to buy permits from cleaner competitors. This is a textbook supply-side tool — rather than the state directly dictating how firms should produce, it changes the relative price of polluting versus clean production, incentivising firms to innovate their way towards cleaner methods of production as part of ordinary cost-minimising behaviour, reconciling continued economic activity with a genuine, legally-binding downward trajectory for emissions.

Conflict 6: Growth & Employment vs Government Finances

Expansionary fiscal policy — used to boost growth and reduce unemployment in the short run (Section 10) — directly worsens the budget deficit and national debt (see the BIZ-OMICS Fiscal Policy guide's crowding-out and national debt sections), creating a genuine conflict between the growth/employment objectives and the sustainability of government finances.

Government raises spending and/or cuts taxes to boost growth and reduce unemployment → the budget deficit widens → government must borrow more, issuing additional gilts → national debt rises as a share of GDP → if markets judge the resulting debt trajectory unsustainable, gilt yields can rise sharply, raising the government's future borrowing costs and potentially forcing a disruptive change of course → growth and employment may have improved in the short run, but government finances have deteriorated, constraining future policy choices.

Supply-Side Reconciliation: because supply-side policy raises long-run potential output (LRAS) without requiring ongoing demand-side deficit spending, successful supply-side reform can improve growth and the government's fiscal position simultaneously — a larger, more productive economy generates more tax revenue at unchanged tax rates, easing the deficit without the government needing to choose between growth and fiscal discipline. This is precisely the logic behind fiscal rules that distinguish current spending from capital investment (see the BIZ-OMICS Fiscal Policy guide's fiscal rules section) — allowing government borrowing specifically to fund supply-side investment (infrastructure, R&D, skills) on the basis that it should pay for itself over time through a larger future tax base, while still requiring day-to-day spending to be met from current revenue.

★ REAL WORLD: COVID-19 Support vs the UK's Fiscal Position
The furlough scheme and business support measures deployed from March 2020 successfully protected millions of jobs and cushioned the depth of the COVID-19 recession, directly serving the growth and employment objectives. But this came at a substantial fiscal cost: the UK budget deficit reached a peacetime record of around 15% of GDP in 2020/21, and the national debt rose above 100% of GDP for the first time since the early 1960s — a clear illustration of the short-run trade-off between protecting growth/employment and the sustainability of government finances, and one that has directly shaped the tighter fiscal rules introduced by subsequent Chancellors.

Conflict 7: Growth vs Inequality & Living Standards

Aggregate GDP growth does not automatically translate into improved living standards for everyone — it depends heavily on how the gains from growth are distributed. A rising "average" can coexist with stagnant or falling living standards for a large share of the population if the gains are concentrated among a narrow group.

The economy grows, raising aggregate GDP and average income → but if the gains from growth accrue disproportionately to capital owners, high earners, or specific regions/sectors → median and lower incomes may grow much more slowly than the average, or stagnate entirely → income and wealth inequality widens even as the growth objective is met → living standards for those left behind fail to improve in line with the headline growth figures, creating a conflict between the growth objective and broader measures of societal wellbeing.

Some free-market supply-side policies that boost aggregate growth and employment — deregulation, weaker trade union bargaining power, more flexible (and less secure) employment contracts — can simultaneously widen inequality as a side effect, since they tend to shift bargaining power from workers towards capital owners (see the BIZ-OMICS Unemployment guide's discussion of zero-hours contracts and the Cameron/Osborne era). This is precisely why growth and inequality are often framed as being in tension, particularly under a purely free-market supply-side approach.

Not All Supply-Side Policy Worsens Inequality: the conflict is not universal — it depends on which supply-side policies are chosen. Education and skills investment, in particular, can raise growth (via a more productive workforce, shifting LRAS right) and reduce inequality simultaneously, since it directly addresses the skill-based wage premium that drives much of modern income inequality, rather than relying purely on deregulation or weakened worker protections.
★ REAL WORLD: The Nordic Model — Growth and Equality Together
The Nordic economies (Sweden, Denmark, Norway, Finland) consistently rank among the world's most open, flexible, and business-friendly economies on standard "ease of doing business" measures, while simultaneously maintaining some of the world's lowest levels of income inequality (Gini coefficients) and strongest welfare states. Sweden's Rehn-Meidner model (see the BIZ-OMICS Unemployment guide) is a direct illustration: its "solidarity wage" policy deliberately compressed wage differentials between firms and sectors while an extensive active labour market policy retrained and reallocated displaced workers into more productive employment — reconciling growth and labour market flexibility with much lower inequality than in many comparably flexible economies, showing that growth-friendly supply-side reform and equality are not inherently incompatible.
★ REAL WORLD: UK Regional Inequality and "Levelling Up"
UK-wide growth and employment figures have often masked substantial regional disparities — productivity, wages, and living standards in London and the South East have persistently outpaced large parts of the Midlands, the North, and Wales, a legacy partly rooted in the same deindustrialisation that also drives the UK's current account deficit (Conflict 4). The government's "Levelling Up" agenda from 2019 explicitly framed regional inequality as a policy priority in its own right, proposing supply-side measures — transport infrastructure investment, devolution of powers to regional mayors, and targeted investment in left-behind areas — intended to spread the benefits of national growth more evenly, rather than relying on aggregate GDP growth alone to improve living standards everywhere.
10

Demand-Side Policy: Reconciling Conflicts in the Short Run

The specification requires you to explain how policies "may be used to try to reconcile possible policy conflicts" — note the word "try": demand-side tools cannot eliminate the short-run trade-off, but they can manage it.

Gradualism
Rather than moving the Bank Rate sharply, the MPC raises or cuts rates in small, gradual steps, allowing it to monitor incoming data and avoid overcorrecting — reducing the risk of needlessly large output/employment costs when fighting inflation.
Forward Guidance
By credibly signalling the future path of policy, the central bank can influence expectations and behaviour today without an immediate, disruptive rate change — potentially reducing inflation pressure while limiting the short-run hit to growth and employment (see the companion BIZ-OMICS Monetary Policy guide).
Symmetric, Flexible Inflation Targeting
The MPC's mandate explicitly permits it to look through temporary, supply-driven inflation overshoots rather than slamming the brakes on AD every time the 2% target is missed — sacrificing some short-run inflation control to protect employment and growth.
Coordinated Fiscal & Monetary Policy
If fiscal policy (government spending/tax) and monetary policy pull in the same direction, the size of the demand-side intervention needed from either tool alone is smaller — reducing the side-effects (e.g. a smaller required rate rise means a smaller hit to investment).
Important Limitation: none of these tools removes the short-run Phillips Curve trade-off — they only change how the conflict is managed: how fast, how far, and who bears the short-run cost. As shown in Section 9 (Conflict 2), when the economy is away from the NAIRU due to a demand or supply shock, some trade-off between inflation and unemployment/output stabilisation is very difficult to avoid entirely in the short run.
★ REAL WORLD: The 2010–16 Policy Mismatch
As detailed in the companion BIZ-OMICS Monetary Policy guide, UK monetary policy was highly expansionary after 2009 (near-zero Bank Rate, large-scale QE) at the same time fiscal policy turned sharply contractionary (austerity — see the companion BIZ-OMICS Fiscal Policy guide). This illustrates how a lack of coordination between the two arms of demand management can worsen short-run conflicts rather than reconcile them — the Bank was trying to support growth and employment while the Treasury's spending cuts and tax rises pulled in the opposite direction.
11

Supply-Side Policy: Reconciling Conflicts in the Long Run

This is the most important idea in the entire topic for AQA's evaluation marks: in the long run, with a healthy supply side, it is in principle possible to achieve all four macroeconomic objectives simultaneously. Supply-side policy is what makes this possible, because it tackles the root causes of unemployment and inflation rather than just managing the symptoms via demand.

Why Supply-Side Policy Is Different

Demand-side policy (Section 10) shifts AD and therefore moves the economy along a given Phillips Curve / SRAS curve — it cannot change the position of the LRPC or LRAS. Supply-side policy instead shifts LRAS to the right and the LRPC to the left (reduces the NAIRU) — meaning the economy can sustainably enjoy both lower unemployment and low inflation at the same time, without the trade-off Friedman described.

PolicyMechanism
Education & TrainingImproves the skills match between job seekers and vacancies, reducing structural unemployment and the NAIRU, and raising labour productivity (shifting LRAS right).
Labour Market FlexibilityReforms that make it easier to hire and reallocate workers (e.g. reduced regulation, more flexible contracts) reduce frictional and structural unemployment, attracting more people into active job search and reducing occupational immobility.
Investment IncentivesTax breaks, R&D credits, and infrastructure spending raise the economy's capital stock and productive capacity, shifting LRAS right and raising potential output without demand-pull inflation.
Competition PolicyReducing barriers to entry and breaking up monopolistic pricing power can keep inflationary pressure lower at any given level of demand, while encouraging firms to invest and expand (supporting employment and growth together).
Fig 11.1 — Successful supply-side policy shifts the LRPC left from LRPC1 to LRPC2, and the whole SRPC shifts down and left with it (SRPC1 → SRPC2). Both curves cross their own LRPC at the same 4% inflation rate — the economy can now sustain a lower rate of unemployment at the same long-run inflation rate, reconciling the unemployment and inflation objectives.
The Golden Sentence for 25-Mark Essays: "While there is a short-run trade-off between unemployment and inflation captured by the Phillips Curve, successful supply-side policy can shift the long-run Phillips Curve (and LRAS) so that lower unemployment becomes sustainable without higher inflation — meaning the conflict between objectives is fundamentally a short-run phenomenon, not a permanent feature of the economy."
★ REAL WORLD: UK Labour Market Reforms, 1980s–2000s
Many economists attribute the UK's falling NAIRU since the 1980s — from estimates around 8–10% to closer to 4–5% by the 2010s — to a combination of trade union reform, the National Minimum Wage's careful calibration, welfare-to-work programmes (e.g. the New Deal, Universal Credit's work incentives), and greater labour market flexibility, all of which reduced frictional and structural unemployment without generating additional inflationary pressure.
★ REAL WORLD: The Netherlands' Wassenaar Agreement (1982) — The "Dutch Miracle"
Facing rising unemployment and high inflation in the early 1980s, the Dutch government, trade unions, and employers' associations signed the Wassenaar Agreement — a tripartite deal in which unions accepted sustained wage moderation in exchange for shorter working hours and a stronger commitment to job creation. Because the deal was negotiated cooperatively rather than imposed, it avoided the destructive wage-price spiral that wage restraint imposed by force often provokes. Dutch unemployment fell from over 10% in the early 1980s to below 3% by the late 1990s, while inflation remained low and stable throughout — a rare case of both objectives improving together, achieved by directly addressing the labour market institutions (union bargaining behaviour) that determine the NAIRU, rather than managing demand.
★ REAL WORLD: Ireland's "Celtic Tiger" (1990s–2000s)
Ireland transformed from one of Western Europe's poorest economies into one of its fastest-growing through a deliberate supply-side strategy: a low 12.5% corporation tax rate to attract foreign direct investment, heavy public investment in education (creating a large pool of skilled, English-speaking workers attractive to multinational employers), and a series of "social partnership" agreements between government, unions, and employers that moderated wage growth in exchange for tax cuts and investment commitments. Unemployment fell from around 15% in the late 1980s to under 4% by 2000, while inflation remained broadly contained for much of the period — illustrating how a coordinated package of supply-side measures, rather than any single policy, can shift both LRAS and the LRPC simultaneously. (The later 2000s did see Ireland's economy overheat via a credit- and property-driven boom, a useful reminder that supply-side gains can still be undone by a subsequent demand-side conflict if left unmanaged.)
★ REAL WORLD: New Zealand's Reserve Bank Act (1989) — Pioneering Inflation Targeting
New Zealand became the first country in the world to adopt a formal, legislated inflation target, requiring its central bank to pursue price stability as a primary, explicitly-defined objective. While this is a monetary rather than supply-side policy in the strictest sense, it is highly relevant here because — as Section 12's fourth scenario explains — credibly anchoring inflation expectations at a low level effectively shifts the entire short-run Phillips Curve downward, allowing lower unemployment to be pursued without a corresponding rise in inflation. New Zealand's early adoption influenced the Bank of England's own move to inflation targeting in 1992 and operational independence in 1997 (see the companion BIZ-OMICS Monetary Policy guide), and is frequently cited as a key reason credible central banks since the 1990s have achieved better joint outcomes on inflation and unemployment than in the volatile 1970s and 1980s.
12

Six Reasons the "Inevitable Trade-Off" Breaks Down

Exam questions love the word "inevitably" (e.g. "Higher unemployment will inevitably result in lower inflation. Discuss."). Don't be unnerved — this is an invitation to show that the simple trade-off does not always hold. Here are six scenarios examiners reward you for recognising.

1
A large negative output gap with Keynesian unemployment

If there is significant spare capacity (a deep recession), the SRAS curve is likely to be highly elastic (flat) over the relevant range. Boosting AD to reduce unemployment can then increase output and reduce unemployment without inflation rising much at all — especially after a deep slump.

2
A generally flatter short-run Phillips Curve

As discussed in Section 8, if the SRPC has become flatter (due to labour market flexibility, weaker unions, and globalisation), unemployment can fall to much lower levels than before without triggering a significant rise in inflation.

3
Supply-side reforms that reduce the NAIRU

As covered in Section 11, successful supply-side policy shifts the entire vertical Long-Run Phillips Curve to the left. Unemployment falls without inflation rising, because the economy's sustainable, non-inflationary rate of unemployment has genuinely fallen.

4
Reducing and anchoring inflationary expectations

If inflationary expectations can be reduced and kept "anchored" at a low level (e.g. through credible, independent inflation targeting), the entire short-run Phillips Curve shifts downward — inflation will be lower at any given rate of unemployment, without unemployment needing to rise.

5
Benign deflation from a positive supply-side shock

A positive supply-side shock (e.g. falling global commodity prices, technological progress, or cheaper imports from globalisation) shifts SRAS to the right. This reduces both inflation and unemployment at the same time — output rises (creating jobs) while the price level falls or rises more slowly, the complete opposite of the standard trade-off.

6
Negative supply-side shocks are stagflationary

Conversely, a negative supply-side shock (e.g. an oil price spike, as in Section 9, Conflict 2) shifts SRAS left and increases both inflation and unemployment simultaneously — also defying the simple trade-off, but in the unfavourable direction.

Exam Tip: Structure Your "Discuss/Evaluate" Answer Around This List: a strong 25-mark answer should: (1) explain the basic Phillips Curve trade-off so the examiner knows you understand the orthodox view; (2) select 2–3 of the six scenarios above as counter-examples, explained with a diagram or chain of reasoning; (3) reach a reasoned judgement about which scenario best describes the current/given context.
★ REAL WORLD: The "China Effect" — Benign Deflation in Practice
From the late 1990s through the 2000s, the integration of China and other low-cost manufacturing economies into world trade pushed down the price of many manufactured goods imported into the UK, while UK demand for these cheaper goods supported output and employment in distribution, retail and related services — a real-world example of scenario 5: falling inflation pressure alongside resilient employment.
13

Using AD/AS to Analyse Conflicting Objectives

AQA expects you to use the AD/AS model fluently to analyse the causes of policy conflicts in both the short run and long run. This section brings together Sections 2–5 into a single, exam-ready framework.

FeatureNegative Output GapPositive Output Gap
AD vs LRASAD intersects SRAS to the left of LRASAD intersects SRAS to the right of LRAS
Price levelLower (below the full-capacity price level)Higher (above the full-capacity price level)
UnemploymentAbove the NAIRU — cyclical unemployment presentBelow the NAIRU — labour market overheating
Inflationary pressureFalling / low — risk of deflation if persistentRising — demand-pull inflation
Typical causeRecession; weak consumer/business confidence; tight fiscal or monetary policyCredit boom; loose fiscal or monetary policy; strong export demand
Likely policy responseExpansionary fiscal/monetary policy to close the gapContractionary fiscal/monetary policy to close the gap

Modelling a Conflict: A Negative Supply-Side Shock

To model a genuine conflict (rather than just a demand-driven gap), shift SRAS instead of AD. A leftward shift of SRAS (e.g. from a rise in global energy prices) raises the price level and reduces real output simultaneously — generating higher inflation and higher unemployment together. This is the AD/AS representation of the stagflation conflict from Section 9.

Negative supply shock (e.g. oil price spike) → SRAS shifts left → at the new equilibrium, price level rises (PL1 → PL2) and real output falls (Y1 → Y2, opening a negative output gap) → inflation rises and unemployment rises → the MPC faces the genuine policy conflict described in Section 9, Conflict 2.
Exam Tip: Always Specify Which Curve Is Shifting. A very common error is to shift AD when a question describes a supply-side event (or vice versa). Demand-side conflicts (Sections 2–5) come from AD shifting relative to a fixed LRAS. Genuine stagflation-type conflicts (Section 9) come from SRAS shifting, which moves price level and output in opposite directions from what a simple AD-driven analysis would predict. Naming the correct curve is essential for full marks.
★ REAL WORLD: Modelling COVID-19 with AD/AS
The COVID-19 pandemic is a textbook example of simultaneous AD and SRAS shocks: lockdowns shifted SRAS sharply left (supply chains disrupted, workers unable to work) while uncertainty and restricted spending opportunities shifted AD left too. The initial effect was a deep negative output gap and falling inflation in 2020 — but the subsequent stimulus-driven recovery in demand, combined with persistent supply constraints, helped fuel the inflation surge of 2021–22.
14

Evaluation — Can All Four Objectives Be Met At Once?

A top-mark answer must weigh up both sides: the case that conflicts are a persistent, unavoidable feature of macroeconomic policy, and the case that they are fundamentally short-run and can be reconciled with the right policy mix.

✗ The Case That Conflicts Are Largely Unavoidable

Policy lags

Demand management always works with a lag (typically 18–24 months for monetary policy), so by the time a conflict is recognised, the policy response may arrive too late or overshoot — temporarily worsening one objective while fixing another.

Supply-side policy is slow

Supply-side policies take years, sometimes decades, to lower the NAIRU or raise LRAS meaningfully — they cannot resolve a conflict that is happening now.

Unpredictable shocks

Supply-side shocks (oil prices, pandemics, geopolitical events) are unpredictable and can hit at any time, creating stagflationary conflicts that no amount of structural reform can fully insure against.

Distributional conflicts

Different objectives matter more to different groups in society (savers vs borrowers, exporters vs importers, employed vs unemployed), so even a policy that improves the aggregate picture may create distributional conflicts that feel like trade-offs to those who lose out.

✓ The Case That Conflicts Are Fundamentally Short-Run

No long-run trade-off

The Friedman model shows clearly that in the long run, once expectations adjust, the economy returns to the NAIRU regardless of the inflation rate — there is no permanent trade-off between inflation and unemployment.

Historical counter-examples exist

Historical episodes of falling unemployment and falling inflation occurring together (Section 12, scenarios 2, 3 and 5) show that the trade-off is not "inevitable" even in the short run, given the right supply-side conditions.

Credible institutions help

Credible, independent inflation-targeting institutions (like the Bank of England) have anchored expectations effectively since 1997, reducing the size and persistence of conflicts compared with the volatile 1970s and 1980s.

Supply-side policy targets root causes

Supply-side policy directly targets the root cause of the conflict (the position of LRAS/LRPC) rather than just shuffling the symptoms around with demand management.

A Balanced Conclusion: the strongest essays conclude that policy conflicts are real and significant in the short run, especially following demand or supply shocks, but that they are not a fixed law of economics. The UK's experience — from the 1970s stagflation crisis through to the low-inflation, low-unemployment 2010s, and back to the stagflationary shock of 2022 — shows that the relationship between objectives depends heavily on the state of the supply side, the credibility of policy institutions, and the nature of the shocks the economy faces, rather than being permanently fixed.
★ REAL WORLD: The "Goldilocks Economy" — Late 1990s US
The US economy of the late 1990s is often cited as a rare period where unemployment fell to around 4% while inflation remained low and stable — economists at the time credited productivity gains from information technology (a positive supply-side shock raising LRAS) for allowing this unusually favourable combination, illustrating that "all four objectives at once" is achievable, at least temporarily, under the right supply-side conditions.
Evaluation Checklist for 25-Mark Questions: 1. Define the objectives and the relevant conflict precisely. 2. Use an AD/AS or Phillips Curve diagram to show the short-run mechanism. 3. Identify at least one real-world example. 4. Use Section 12's six scenarios to challenge the word "inevitably" if it appears. 5. Distinguish short-run from long-run explicitly. 6. Reach a final judgement — don't just list both sides without concluding.
15

True or False — Check Your Understanding

Use this as a self-test: read each statement, decide whether it's true or false, and jot your answer down before checking. Click the button below to reveal the correct answers and explanations once you've had a go — or print the page now, while answers are hidden, for a blank worksheet version.

#StatementYour AnswerT/FExplanation
1A negative output gap means actual output is below potential output.TRUEBy definition, a negative output gap occurs when actual real GDP is lower than the economy's sustainable, full-capacity (potential) output.
2A positive output gap is typically associated with falling inflation.FALSEA positive output gap means the economy is overheating — demand exceeds sustainable capacity, generating demand-pull inflation, not falling inflation.
3The NAIRU stands for the Non-Accelerating Inflation Rate of Unemployment.TRUEThe NAIRU is the rate of unemployment consistent with stable (neither accelerating nor decelerating) inflation.
4A.W. Phillips found a positive relationship between inflation and unemployment.FALSEPhillips found an inverse (negative) relationship — higher unemployment was associated with lower wage inflation, and vice versa.
5The Long-Run Phillips Curve is vertical at the NAIRU.TRUEBecause in the long run the economy always returns to the NAIRU regardless of the inflation rate, the LRPC is drawn as a vertical line at that rate of unemployment.
6According to Friedman, there is a permanent, exploitable trade-off between inflation and unemployment.FALSEFriedman argued the trade-off only exists in the short run, while expectations adjust. In the long run there is no trade-off.
7Money illusion means workers correctly perceive that their real wage has not changed.FALSEMoney illusion is the mistaken belief that a rise in money wages represents a rise in real wages — workers initially fail to realise prices have risen by the same amount.
8A rise in inflationary expectations shifts the short-run Phillips Curve upward.TRUEEach short-run Phillips Curve corresponds to a given level of expected inflation; higher expected inflation shifts the whole curve upward.
9Supply-side policy can reduce the NAIRU, shifting the LRPC to the left.TRUEPolicies that reduce frictional and structural unemployment (training, labour market flexibility) lower the equilibrium (NAIRU) rate of unemployment, shifting the vertical LRPC leftward.
10Demand-side policy can permanently shift the position of the LRPC.FALSEDemand-side policy moves the economy along a given short-run Phillips Curve, or between short-run curves via expectations — it cannot shift the vertical LRPC.
11A negative supply-side shock (e.g. an oil price spike) raises both inflation and unemployment.TRUEA leftward shift in SRAS raises the price level while reducing real output (and therefore raising unemployment) — the classic stagflation scenario.
12A positive supply-side shock can reduce both inflation and unemployment simultaneously.TRUEA rightward shift in SRAS (e.g. falling commodity prices, technological progress) lowers the price level while raising real output and employment — "benign deflation".
13If the short-run Phillips Curve is very flat, unemployment can fall a long way before inflation accelerates much.TRUEA flatter SRPC means a given fall in unemployment produces a smaller rise in inflation than it would along a steeper curve.
14Stagflation refers to rising inflation combined with falling unemployment.FALSEStagflation refers to rising inflation combined with stagnant growth and rising (not falling) unemployment — the opposite of the simple Phillips Curve trade-off.
15In the long run, with a healthy supply side, it is in principle possible to achieve all four macroeconomic objectives together.TRUEThis is the central long-run message of the topic: supply-side health allows growth, low unemployment, low inflation and balance of payments equilibrium to be pursued together.
16Cyclical unemployment is best explained by a negative output gap.TRUECyclical (demand-deficient) unemployment arises specifically because actual output is below potential output, meaning firms need fewer workers than the workforce can supply.
17The Bank of England's mandate requires it to ignore unemployment and growth entirely when setting interest rates.FALSEThe MPC's primary objective is price stability, but subject to meeting that target it is also required to support the government's objectives for growth and employment.
18An "inevitably" exam question is inviting you to simply agree without qualification.FALSEWords like "inevitably" are deliberate invitations to challenge the absolute claim and explore counter-examples (see Section 12) before reaching a balanced judgement.
19A demand-led boom that raises growth and lowers unemployment tends to widen the current account deficit.TRUERising domestic incomes raise demand for imports; if export growth doesn't keep pace, the trade balance worsens even as growth and employment improve.
20Export-led growth reconciles the growth objective with the balance of payments objective more easily than consumption-led growth.TRUEGrowth driven by rising net exports raises GDP and employment while improving (rather than worsening) the current account, avoiding the standard growth-vs-BoP trade-off.
21Germany ran a larger current account deficit than the UK in 2024.FALSEGermany recorded the largest current account surplus in the G7 in 2024 (5.8% of GDP), while the UK ran a deficit (2.2% of GDP) — the opposite of the statement.
22Carbon pricing (e.g. the EU Emissions Trading Scheme) is an example of supply-side policy reconciling growth with environmental objectives.TRUECarbon pricing changes the relative cost of polluting vs clean production, incentivising firms to innovate towards cleaner methods as part of ordinary profit-maximising behaviour, rather than dictating production methods directly.
23Expansionary fiscal policy used to boost growth and employment has no effect on government finances.FALSEExpansionary fiscal policy directly widens the budget deficit and increases the national debt, creating a genuine conflict with the objective of sustainable government finances.
24All supply-side policies that boost growth necessarily worsen inequality.FALSEWhile some free-market supply-side policies (deregulation, weaker union power) can worsen inequality as a side effect, others — particularly education and skills investment — can raise growth and reduce inequality simultaneously.
16

Exam Technique & Model Chains of Reasoning

The Golden Chain: Explaining a Demand-Pull Conflict

AD rises faster than potential output → positive output gap opens → firms draw in scarce labour → unemployment falls below the NAIRU → wages and input costs rise as resources become scarce → firms pass on costs as higher prices → demand-pull inflation rises above target → MPC faces a conflict between the low-unemployment outcome (good) and the inflation outcome (bad) → MPC may raise the Bank Rate, which closes the gap but raises unemployment back up.

The Golden Chain: Explaining a Negative Supply-Side Shock (Stagflation)

Negative supply shock occurs (e.g. oil price spike) → SRAS shifts left → at the new short-run equilibrium, price level rises → real output falls, opening a negative output gap → unemployment rises → inflation and unemployment rise together → MPC faces a genuine policy conflict: raising rates to fight inflation would deepen the negative output gap and raise unemployment further, while holding rates protects employment but allows inflation to persist above target.

The Golden Chain: Friedman's Expectations-Augmented Model (A → F)

Economy starts at the NAIRU with stable inflation and 0% expected inflation (point A) → AD rises, positive output gap opens → unemployment falls below NAIRU as workers experience money illusion (point B, along Ph0) → in the long run workers realise real wages haven't risen → unemployment returns to NAIRU, but inflationary expectations have risen, shifting the curve up to Ph1 (point C) → the process can repeat, pushing the economy to point D then E on ever-higher curves → reducing inflation back down requires accepting temporarily higher unemployment than the NAIRU (point F) until expectations adjust down again.

The Golden Chain: Supply-Side Policy Reconciling the Conflict

Government introduces supply-side policy (e.g. training schemes, labour market reform, investment incentives) → frictional and structural unemployment fall → the NAIRU falls, shifting the vertical LRPC left → LRAS shifts right, raising potential output → the economy can sustain a lower rate of unemployment at the same rate of inflation → growth, employment and price stability objectives are reconciled in the long run, without the short-run trade-off that demand-side policy alone could not avoid.

Key Exam Tips

1

Always define the output gap before analysing it. State explicitly whether you mean a positive or negative gap, and link it to both AD/AS and the labour market — don't assume the examiner infers the connection.

2

Distinguish short run from long run explicitly. Use the words "in the short run..." and "however, in the long run..." as signal phrases — AQA mark schemes reward this distinction heavily on this topic.

3

Name the correct curve that is shifting. AD-driven conflicts (Sections 2–5) shift AD against a fixed LRAS. Supply-shock conflicts (Section 9, Conflict 2) shift SRAS. Confusing the two is a common, costly error.

4

Use a diagram wherever possible. An AD/AS or Phillips Curve diagram, correctly labelled (axes, curves, equilibrium points, and the gap itself), can earn marks that a purely written answer cannot.

5

Challenge "inevitably" and "always" directly. Use the six scenarios from Section 12 as a ready-made bank of counter-examples for any question implying an absolute, unbreakable trade-off.

6

Evaluate — never just describe. For 25-mark questions, you must reach a final, justified judgement about which view is more convincing given the context in the question, not simply present both sides and stop.

7

Real-world examples earn marks. Reference specific episodes: 1970s stagflation, 2008–09 negative output gap, pre-COVID low unemployment/low inflation, the 2022 energy price shock, and UK supply-side reforms since the 1980s.

8

Link back to policy. A strong answer doesn't stop at "this is a conflict" — it goes on to discuss how demand-side policy might manage it in the short run (Section 10) and how supply-side policy might resolve it in the long run (Section 11).

BIZ-OMICS | biz-omics.co.uk | AQA A-Level Economics | Macroeconomic Objective Conflicts — The Complete Guide
